title: Nmap: The Swiss Army Knife 🔪
description: Master the essential flags and options of the industry-standard scanner.

slides.title: Why Nmap is King 👑
slides.content:
# The Network Mapper

**Nmap** (Network Mapper) is the de facto standard for network discovery and security auditing. It's been in development since 1997 and is included in **every** penetration testing distribution.

**Core Capabilities:**
- Host Discovery
- Port Scanning
- Version Detection
- OS Fingerprinting
- Scriptable Interaction (NSE)

**Basic Syntax:**
```bash
nmap [scan type] [options] [target]
```

slides.title: Target Specification 🎯
slides.content:
# Telling Nmap What to Scan

You can specify targets in many flexible ways:

- **Single IP**: `nmap 192.168.1.1`
- **CIDR Notation**: `nmap 192.168.1.0/24` (entire class C)
- **Range**: `nmap 192.168.1.1-50`
- **List**: `nmap 192.168.1.1,15,20`
- **Domain**: `nmap scanme.nmap.org`
- **Exclude**: `nmap 192.168.1.0/24 --exclude 192.168.1.1`
- **From File**: `nmap -iL targets.txt`

quizData.question: Which Nmap command scans only the IP addresses 192.168.1.10, 192.168.1.11, and 192.168.1.12?
quizData.explanation: Correct! The dash indicates a range of octets or full addresses.

slides.title: Essential Nmap Flags (Cheat Sheet) 📋
slides.content:
# Must-Know Options

| Flag | Long Option | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `-sS` | (default) | TCP SYN Scan (Stealth) |
| `-sT` | | TCP Connect Scan |
| `-sU` | | UDP Scan |
| `-sV` | `--version-intensity` | Service Version Detection |
| `-O` | `--osscan-guess` | OS Fingerprinting |
| `-A` | | Aggressive Scan (OS, Version, Scripts, Traceroute) |
| `-p` | `--port` | Specify ports (e.g., `-p 22,80,443` or `-p-` for all) |
| `-T` | `--timing-template` | Timing template (0-5) |
| `-oN` / `-oG` | `--output` | Save results to file |
| `-Pn` | | Skip host discovery (treat all hosts as up) |
| `-n` | | Never do DNS resolution (faster) |

slides.title: Timing Templates ⏱️
slides.content:
# Speed vs. Stealth

Nmap provides **six timing templates** (0 to 5) that control how aggressively it scans.

| Level | Name | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `-T0` | Paranoid | Extremely slow, evades IDS. (One probe every 5 minutes) |
| `-T1` | Sneaky | Very slow, for IDS evasion. |
| `-T2` | Polite | Slower, less bandwidth. |
| `-T3` | **Normal** | **Default.** Good balance. |
| `-T4` | Aggressive | Fast, assumes good network. |
| `-T5` | Insane | Super fast, sacrifices accuracy. |

**Hacker Tip**: Use `-T4` on internal networks. Use `-T2` or `-T1` on external engagements to avoid detection.

slides.title: Output Formats 📄
slides.content: Always save your scan results! Nmap supports multiple output formats for different purposes.
slides.codeSnippet:
# Normal output (human readable)
nmap -oN scan_results.txt 192.168.56.101

# Grepable output (easy parsing)
nmap -oG scan_results.gnmap 192.168.56.101

# XML output (for tools like Metasploit)
nmap -oX scan_results.xml 192.168.56.101

# All formats at once
nmap -oA scan_results 192.168.56.101

quizData.question: You need to scan all 65535 ports on a target as quickly as possible, without caring about detection. Which command is best?
quizData.explanation: `-p-` scans all ports, and `-T5` is the fastest timing template.
